    • Section 2.2 Scikit-learn Random forest model with top 10 important features
    • Section 2.3 SHAP

Section 1 Data Preparation ¶

Handling Missing values: columns with more than 20% missing values are dropped, numeric missing values are imputed with the median, and categorical missing values are imputed with mode.¶
Correlation Analysis: numeric features are analyzed for correlations, and interaction features are created for highly correlated pairs (absolute correlation > 0.5).¶
Feature Encoding: Categorical variables with fewer than 10 unique values are one-hot encoded, and constant or duplicate columns are removed.¶
Shape Comparison: The dataset's shape is compared before and after feature engineering to assess the impact of transformations.¶

In [3]:
# 1. First, let's analyze missing values and correlations
def analyze_features(data):
    # Missing values analysis
    missing_vals = data.isnull().sum().sort_values(ascending=False)
    missing_pcts = (missing_vals / len(data) * 100).round(2)
    missing_df = pd.concat([missing_vals, missing_pcts], axis=1, 
                          keys=['Missing Count', 'Missing %'])
    
    print("Missing Values Analysis:")
    print(missing_df[missing_df['Missing Count'] > 0])
    print("Dropping columns with high missing percentage")
    threshold=0.2
    cols_to_drop = missing_pcts[missing_pcts > threshold].index.tolist()
    # Drop the columns
    data = data.drop(columns=cols_to_drop)
    
    # Correlation analysis for numeric columns
    numeric_cols = data.select_dtypes(include=['int64', 'float64']).columns
    corr_matrix = data[numeric_cols].corr()
    
    # Plot correlation heatmap
    plt.figure(figsize=(12, 8))
    sns.heatmap(corr_matrix, cmap='coolwarm', center=0, annot=False)
    plt.title('Correlation Heatmap')
    plt.show()
    
    return data

# 2. Feature Engineering Function
def engineer_features(data):
    df = data.copy()
    
    # Handle missing values
    numeric_cols = df.select_dtypes(include=['int64', 'float64']).columns
    categorical_cols = df.select_dtypes(include=['object']).columns
    
    # Remove target from numeric columns if present
    numeric_cols = [col for col in numeric_cols if col != 'loan_default']
    
    # Fill numeric missing values with median
    for col in numeric_cols:
        df[col] = df[col].fillna(df[col].median())
    
    # Fill categorical missing values with mode
    for col in categorical_cols:
        df[col] = df[col].fillna(df[col].mode()[0])
    
    # Create interaction features for highly correlated numeric variables
    corr_matrix = df[numeric_cols].corr()
    high_corr_pairs = np.where(np.abs(corr_matrix) > 0.5)
    high_corr_pairs = [(corr_matrix.index[x], corr_matrix.columns[y]) 
                       for x, y in zip(*high_corr_pairs) 
                       if x != y and x < y]  # avoid duplicates
    
    
    # One-hot encoding for categorical variables
    for col in categorical_cols:
        if df[col].nunique() < 10:  # adjust threshold as needed
            df = pd.get_dummies(df, columns=[col], prefix=col)
    
    # Remove any constant or duplicate columns
    nunique = df.nunique()
    cols_to_drop = nunique[nunique == 1].index
    df = df.drop(columns=cols_to_drop)
    
    return df

# 3. Apply feature engineering
print("Analyzing original features...")
data = analyze_features(data)

print("\nApplying feature engineering...")
data = engineer_features(data)

# 4. Compare shapes
print("\nDataset shapes:")
print(f"Original data shape: {data.shape}")
print(f"Engineered data shape: {data.shape}")

Section 2 RF ¶

In [15]:
def VarImp(model_name, m_name):
    from sklearn.metrics import roc_curve, auc
    from sklearn.metrics import average_precision_score
    from sklearn.metrics import precision_recall_curve
    import matplotlib.pyplot as plt
    import pandas as pd
    
    # Get variable importance data
    variables = model_name._model_json['output']['variable_importances']['variable']
    scaled_importance = model_name._model_json['output']['variable_importances']['scaled_importance']
    percentage = model_name._model_json['output']['variable_importances']['percentage']
    
    # Create DataFrame
    var_imp_df = pd.DataFrame({
        'variable': variables,
        'scaled_importance': scaled_importance,
        'percentage': percentage
    }).sort_values('scaled_importance', ascending=False)
    
    # Plot the variable importance
    plt.rcdefaults()
    y_pos = np.arange(len(variables))
    fig, ax = plt.subplots(figsize=(6, len(variables)/2))
    ax.barh(y_pos, scaled_importance, align='center', color='green')
    ax.set_yticks(y_pos)
    ax.set_yticklabels(variables)
    ax.invert_yaxis()
    ax.set_xlabel('Scaled Importance')
    ax.set_title(m_name + ' Variable Importance')
    plt.show()
    
    return var_imp_df

In [21]:
def encode_all_objects(X_train, X_test):
    """
    Automatically detect and encode all object-type columns in both training and test sets
    """
    # Make copies to avoid modifying original data
    X_train_encoded = X_train.copy()
    X_test_encoded = X_test.copy()
    
    # Get list of object columns
    object_columns = X_train.select_dtypes(include=['object']).columns
    
    if len(object_columns) > 0:
        print(f"Found object columns to encode: {list(object_columns)}")
        
        for col in object_columns:
            # Get unique values from both train and test
            all_values = pd.concat([X_train[col], X_test[col]]).unique()
            
            # Create a mapping dictionary
            value_to_id = {val: idx for idx, val in enumerate(all_values)}
            
            # Transform both series using the mapping
            X_train_encoded[col] = X_train[col].map(value_to_id)
            X_test_encoded[col] = X_test[col].map(value_to_id)
            
            print(f"Encoded {col}: {len(value_to_id)} unique values")
    else:
        print("No object columns found")
    
    return X_train_encoded, X_test_encoded

# Use the function
X_train, X_test = encode_all_objects(X_train, X_test)

from sklearn.ensemble import RandomForestClassifier
# Initialize and train the model
sk_rf = RandomForestClassifier(
    n_estimators=500,
    max_depth=40,
    min_samples_leaf=50,
    random_state=1234
)
sk_rf.fit(X_train, y_train)

sk_rf_importances = sk_rf.feature_importances_
sk_rf_indices = np.argsort(sk_rf_importances)

features = X_train.columns
plt.title('Feature Importances')
plt.barh(range(len(sk_rf_indices)), sk_rf_importances[sk_rf_indices], color='b', align='center')
plt.yticks(range(len(sk_rf_indices)), [features[i] for i in sk_rf_indices])
plt.xlabel('Relative Importance')
plt.show()

Found object columns to encode: ['MB007']
Encoded MB007: 49 unique values

ABS Summary Plot

In [28]:
def ABS_SHAP(df_shap,df):
    #import matplotlib as plt
    # Make a copy of the input data
    shap_v = pd.DataFrame(df_shap)
    feature_list = df.columns
    shap_v.columns = feature_list
    df_v = df.copy().reset_index().drop('index',axis=1)
    
    # Determine the correlation in order to plot with different colors
    corr_list = list()
    for i in feature_list:
        b = np.corrcoef(shap_v[i],df_v[i])[1][0]
        corr_list.append(b)
    corr_df = pd.concat([pd.Series(feature_list),pd.Series(corr_list)],axis=1).fillna(0)
    # Make a data frame. Column 1 is the feature, and Column 2 is the correlation coefficient
    corr_df.columns  = ['Variable','Corr']
    corr_df['Sign'] = np.where(corr_df['Corr']>0,'red','blue')
    
    # Plot it
    shap_abs = np.abs(shap_v)
    k=pd.DataFrame(shap_abs.mean()).reset_index()
    k.columns = ['Variable','SHAP_abs']
    k2 = k.merge(corr_df,left_on = 'Variable',right_on='Variable',how='inner')
    k2 = k2.sort_values(by='SHAP_abs',ascending = True)
    colorlist = k2['Sign']
    ax = k2.plot.barh(x='Variable',y='SHAP_abs',color = colorlist, figsize=(6,4),legend=False)
    ax.set_xlabel("SHAP Value (Red = Positive Impact)")
    
ABS_SHAP(shap_values_class1,X_train)  
ABS_SHAP(shap_values_class0,X_train)
TOP 5 important features for target variable are AP003, TD013, TD009, AP004, and TD014. AP003 shows a negative relationship with target varibale default = 1, respectively, shows a positive relationship with target variable default = 0. TD013, TD009, AP004, TD014 show a positive relationship with target varaible defauly = 1, and negative relationship with target variable default = 0.¶
In [29]:
shap.summary_plot(shap_values_class1, X_train)
shap.summary_plot(shap_values_class0, X_train)
Feature importance: Those Variables are ranked in descending order.¶
Impact: The horizontal location shows whether the effect of that value is associated with a higher or lower prediction.¶
Original value: Color shows whether that variable is high (in red) or low (in blue) for that observation.¶
Correlation: A high level of AP003 content has a high and negative impact on the target varibale default = 1. The “high” comes from the red color, and the negative impact is shown on the X-axis. TD013, TD009, AP004, TD014 content has a high positive impact on the target variable default = 1. The High comes from the red color, and the positive impact is shown on the X-axis.¶
All in all, the default = 1 and default = 0 are symmetric on two graphs.¶

The dependence_plot

In [30]:
for i in X_train.columns:
    # Create a figure with 1 row and 2 columns
    fig, (ax1, ax2) = plt.subplots(1, 2, figsize=(15, 5))
    
    # Plot class 1 on the left subplot
    plt.sca(ax1)
    shap.dependence_plot(i, shap_values_class1, X_train, ax=ax1, show=False)
    ax1.set_title(f'Class 1 - {i}')
    
    # Plot class 0 on the right subplot
    plt.sca(ax2)
    shap.dependence_plot(i, shap_values_class0, X_train, ax=ax2, show=False)
    ax2.set_title(f'Class 0 - {i}')
    
    # Adjust layout to prevent overlap
    plt.tight_layout()
    plt.show()
In [31]:
shap_interaction_values = shap.TreeExplainer(sk_rf).shap_interaction_values(X_train.iloc[:100,:])
# Select positive class (class 1) interactions
interaction_values_class1 = shap_interaction_values[:, :, :, 1]  # Shape will be (100, 10, 10)

# Or for negative class (class 0)
interaction_values_class0 = shap_interaction_values[:, :, :, 0]  # Shape will be (100, 10, 10)

# Create summary plot for class 1
shap.summary_plot(interaction_values_class1, X_train.iloc[:100,:])
shap.summary_plot(interaction_values_class0, X_train.iloc[:100,:])

The force_plot for 4 individual observations

In [32]:
X_output = X_test.copy()
X_output.loc[:,'predict'] = np.round(sk_rf.predict(X_output),2)

In [33]:
shap.initjs()
In [34]:
def shap_plot(j,class01=1):
    explainerModel = shap.TreeExplainer(sk_rf)
    shap_values_Model = explainerModel.shap_values(X_output)
    
    # Ensure the expected value is accessed correctly
    expected_value = explainerModel.expected_value[class01]
    shap_value_for_instance = shap_values_Model[j,:,class01]
    # print("Shape of SHAP values for class 1:", shap_values_Model[j,:,class01].shape)
    # print("Shape of instance:", X_output.iloc[[j]].shape)
    # Create the force plot
    p = shap.force_plot(expected_value, shap_value_for_instance, X_output.iloc[[j]])
    return p
